stories.headline: Trump cancels planned Iran strikes, claims war 'settled'; says a memorandum could be signed within days
stories.summary: President Trump abruptly canceled airstrikes he had threatened hours earlier and said the US had reached 'a great settlement of the war with Iran,' with a 'conceptual' memorandum possibly signed in Europe within days. Iran's Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmail Baghaei called the reports 'mere speculation,' saying no final decision had been reached, while Israel said it is not a party. The reversal followed a second day of mutual strikes and Iranian attacks on US Gulf bases.

perspectives.keyFraming: Whiplash diplomacy: Trump went from threatening to seize Iran's oil hub to declaring the war settled within hours, with no Iranian confirmation.
perspectives.summary: Trump said he canceled the scheduled strikes because discussions had been 'brought to the highest level of Iranian leadership and approved,' and that a 'very strong memorandum of understanding that is a little conceptual' could be signed 'maybe in Europe' within days. He claimed the Strait of Hormuz would reopen and the naval blockade lift on signing, while Iran had not confirmed any terms.

sources.title: Trump now says a peace deal will be announced 'soon,' cancels further strikes
Iran live updates: Trump says agreement with Iran could be signed as early as this weekend

perspectives.keyFraming: European and Gulf outlets cast the reversal as pressure-driven, coming after fresh Iranian attacks on US bases rather than a clear US victory.
perspectives.summary: The Washington Post reported Trump withdrew his threat to seize Iran's Kharg Island oil terminal after Iran struck up to five US bases, with verified Iranian imagery showing damage to 228 or more structures. Coverage framed the about-face as a de-escalation forced by the costs of escalation, while cautioning that earlier 2026 ceasefires had collapsed.

sources.title: Trump says he has canceled plans to strike Iran, claiming deal is near
Live updates: Trump says US 'ended the war with Iran,' though Tehran has yet to confirm a deal

perspectives.keyFraming: Trump and allies cast the settlement as Iran capitulating after a sustained US 'pounding,' though GOP hawks demand congressional review and removal of Iran's enriched uranium.
perspectives.summary: Fox News reported Trump canceled the Thursday-evening strikes after talks reached Iran's top leadership, saying Iran agreed never to obtain a nuclear weapon — the campaign's central aim. Asked why now, he said 'because they've taken a pounding.' Sen. Lindsey Graham urged that any deal be 'presented to Congress for review and approval' and that Iran's highly enriched uranium be removed.

sources.title: Trump says he's canceled Iran strikes, adds potential deal-signing 'to be announced shortly'
The Memo: Trump announces 'great settlement' on Iran, but the world awaits details

perspectives.keyFraming: Tehran publicly rejects Trump's characterization, casting Washington as the unreliable party introducing last-minute demands.
perspectives.summary: Iran's Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmail Baghaei said reports of a finalized agreement were 'mere speculation' and that no final decision had been reached, accusing the US of 'excessive demands' and 'new requests.' Iranian outlets said much of a draft had been agreed but that Tehran would not 'give way under pressure,' leaving Trump's settlement claim unconfirmed by the other principal party.

sources.title: Iran says no final decision on any deal with US, calls reports 'mere speculation'
Trump says US, Iran to sign peace deal in Europe this weekend; Tehran rejects his claims

stories.headline: Trump touts 'great settlement' with Iran and a weekend signing in Europe to reopen Hormuz; Tehran disputes any final deal
stories.summary: President Trump said the US had 'made a great settlement of the war' with Iran and that a signing could come over the weekend in Europe, with the Strait of Hormuz reopening once a memorandum is finalized. Iran's Foreign Ministry said no final agreement had been concluded and that the strait remained under regional management, directly disputing Trump's framing. The US naval blockade stays in force pending any signing.

perspectives.keyFraming: Washington frames an imminent deal that reopens Hormuz, while the terms remain unsigned and contested.
perspectives.summary: Reuters and CNN reported Trump said the war was settled 'subject to finalization,' expecting a signing within days in Europe that would reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Officials cautioned the text was not final, and Trump said the naval blockade would remain until any agreement is signed.

sources.title: Trump says US and Iran reached a settlement; signing expected soon
Trump says 'great settlement' on Iran to be signed soon and strait will open

perspectives.keyFraming: Coverage stresses Tehran's denial that any settlement is final and skepticism about the announcement's substance.
perspectives.summary: NPR reported Trump canceled planned strikes and promised a deal 'soon,' while NBC News quoted Iranian officials saying no final decision had been reached, underscoring the gap between the US announcement and Tehran's position after weeks of strikes.

sources.title: Trump now says a peace deal will be announced 'soon,' cancels further strikes
Live updates: US, Iran, Trump deal, war, Hormuz

perspectives.keyFraming: European-facing outlets note the proposed signing venue in Europe and the unresolved status of Iran's commitments.
perspectives.summary: RFE/RL reported Trump announced a 'great settlement' to be signed 'quickly,' possibly in Europe, while details on enrichment levels, Hormuz access and sanctions relief remained unspecified and Iranian officials declined to confirm any agreement.

sources.title: Trump announces 'great settlement' reached with Iran, says to be signed 'quickly'

perspectives.keyFraming: Israel says it is not a party to the emerging deal even as it credits US assurances on Iran's nuclear program.
perspectives.summary: Israeli officials said Israel is not party to the emerging US-Iran agreement, with Prime Minister Netanyahu praising Trump's stated commitment to preventing an Iranian nuclear weapon while stressing Israel did not sign any memorandum.

sources.title: Netanyahu says Israel not party to Iran deal but praises Trump for commitment to thwarting nukes

stories.headline: US forces down two Iranian drones near the Strait of Hormuz after Iran fires on a transiting vessel
stories.summary: Early June 12, US forces shot down two Iranian attack drones near the Strait of Hormuz after Iranian units fired on a ship transiting the chokepoint, a fresh incident that tested the truce Trump had announced hours earlier. The episode underscored that the waterway remained contested even as Washington touted an imminent settlement.

perspectives.keyFraming: Reported as a discrete military incident that punctures the de-escalation narrative.
perspectives.summary: RFE/RL reported the US military downed two Iranian drones near the Strait of Hormuz after Iranian forces fired on a transiting vessel, a clash that came as Trump claimed a settlement and the naval blockade stayed in force pending a signing.

sources.title: Iran war: US, Hormuz, oil blockade in the Gulf

perspectives.keyFraming: Framed within Tehran's insistence that nothing is settled and the strait stays under its control.
perspectives.summary: NBC News's liveblog placed the drone incident alongside Iranian statements that no final deal had been reached and that the Strait of Hormuz remained under Iranian and regional management, highlighting the fragility of the announced truce.

sources.title: Live updates: US, Iran, drones, Trump deal, war, Hormuz

stories.headline: Ukrainian drones strike Tatarstan refineries and a Samara petrochemical plant on Russia Day
stories.summary: Ukraine launched a large overnight drone assault June 12, striking the Taneco and TAIF-NK refineries in Tatarstan — more than 1,100 km from the front — and the Togliattikauchuk plant in Samara, with reports of damaged units and a tank-farm fire. A drone hit a residential building in Nizhnekamsk, injuring three, and the city canceled its Russia Day events.

perspectives.keyFraming: Wire coverage focuses on the disruption to Russia Day and the reach of the strikes.
perspectives.summary: Reuters reported the Russian city of Nizhnekamsk canceled public Russia Day events after a drone threat, as Ukrainian long-range strikes reached deep into Tatarstan and Samara, hitting refining and petrochemical infrastructure far from the front line.

sources.title: Russian city of Nizhnekamsk cancels public events after drone threat

perspectives.keyFraming: Independent Russian-exile outlets emphasize the targeting of energy infrastructure deep inside Russia.
perspectives.summary: The Moscow Times reported Ukrainian drone attacks targeted the Taneco and TAIF-NK refineries in Tatarstan and the Togliattikauchuk petrochemical plant in Samara on Russia Day, with damage to processing units and a tank farm and a residential building struck in Nizhnekamsk.

sources.title: Ukrainian drone attacks target Tatarstan refineries and Samara petrochemical plant on Russia Day

perspectives.keyFraming: Kyiv frames the strikes as legitimate pressure on Russia's war economy.
perspectives.summary: Kyiv Post, citing Ukraine's General Staff, reported a large overnight drone operation that struck Russian refining and petrochemical sites deep in the rear, part of Kyiv's sustained campaign to degrade the fuel and revenue that sustain Russia's war effort.

sources.title: Ukraine's drones hit Tatarstan refineries in deep strike on Russia Day

stories.headline: Pakistan says 'final text' of US-Iran war deal reached; Trump disputes leaked terms as Tehran says no final decision
stories.summary: Pakistani PM Shehbaz Sharif said June 12 that mediators reached a 'final, agreed-upon text' to end the US-Iran war, with a possible signing in Geneva as soon as Sunday. Iranian state media described a 14-point draft reopening the Strait of Hormuz within 30 days, releasing about $24bn in frozen assets and waiving oil sanctions during a 60-day talks window. Trump rejected the leaked Iranian terms as bearing 'no relation' to what was agreed, and Tehran said it had reached no final decision.

perspectives.keyFraming: A deal text is reportedly reached, yet Washington and Tehran publicly contradict each other on its substance.
perspectives.summary: Al Jazeera and NBC News reported the mediator's confirmation of an agreed text even as the two governments openly disputed the terms. A US official said the deal would dismantle Iran's nuclear program and reopen Hormuz, while Iran insisted nothing was finalized and the signing venue and timing remained unconfirmed.

sources.title: Pakistan says 'final, agreed upon' text of Iran war ceasefire deal reached
Live updates: Pakistan says U.S.-Iran deal text reached; Trump hits out at Iranian reports
Proposed Iran-U.S. deal would reopen Hormuz strait and lift oil sanctions, Iran state media says

perspectives.keyFraming: Iranian and Russian state outlets stress Tehran made no compromise on red lines and blame US contradictions for the turbulence.
perspectives.summary: RT amplified Iran's foreign ministry saying no final agreement exists and that 'contradictory' US positions have repeatedly disrupted the process. Iranian media detailed the draft's roughly $24bn asset release and oil-sanctions waivers while insisting the nuclear program was untouched.

sources.title: No final agreement on deal with US – Iran's Foreign Ministry
Iran media says draft US deal sees release of $24 billion frozen assets

perspectives.keyFraming: Western outlets treat it as a tentative breakthrough contingent on a signing that has not happened.
perspectives.summary: RFE/RL reported Trump accusing Iran of leaking false details of the proposed deal, underscoring how fragile the announced settlement remains. A Geneva signing was floated for the weekend, with Vice President Vance expected to represent the US, but no text had been signed.

sources.title: Trump Accuses Iran Of Leaking False Details Of Proposed Nuclear Deal

stories.headline: Sunday arrives with no US-Iran signing in Geneva as the uranium standoff clouds the deal's terms
stories.summary: The Sunday June 14 date Trump set for signing the US-Iran 'Islamabad Declaration' in Geneva (VP Vance and Iran's speaker Qalibaf) passed without a signature, with Iran's foreign ministry holding it would not be signed Sunday — possibly remotely 'in the coming days' — citing US 'instability.' The draft would reopen the Strait of Hormuz within 30 days, but Iran's near-weapons-grade uranium remains unresolved: Trump wants it removed while Tehran says it will keep the stockpile.

perspectives.keyFraming: A memorandum is close, but Iran has tempered Trump's confident Sunday-signing timeline and the hardest nuclear questions remain unresolved.
perspectives.summary: Trump said the deal was 'scheduled to be signed' Sunday in Geneva (VP Vance and Iran's speaker Qalibaf), waiving oil sanctions, releasing ~$24bn in frozen funds and reopening Hormuz within 30 days. Hours later Iran's foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baghaei said it would not be signed Sunday but could be finalized 'in the coming days,' possibly electronically, citing reservations about US 'instability'; reported terms appeared to favour Tehran.

sources.title: Iran says signing of memorandum of understanding with US 'will not be tomorrow'
U.S. and Iran signal peace deal close as reports suggest terms appear to favour Tehran

perspectives.keyFraming: Israel publicly distances itself while the central dispute — removing Iran's highly enriched uranium — stays unresolved.
perspectives.summary: The Times of Israel reported a senior US official saying the pending deal 'leads to' the US obtaining Iran's enriched material, while Trump conceded the MoU only 'conceptually' addresses the nuclear file. Netanyahu's office said Israel is not a party to the deal, and Iranian sources said Tehran had decided to keep its near-weapons-grade uranium inside the country, defying a core US demand.

sources.title: Iranian sources say regime has decided to keep highly enriched uranium in Iran, defying Trump

perspectives.keyFraming: Tehran insists no deal is final and that any agreement must also end the fighting in Lebanon.
perspectives.summary: Foreign Minister Araghchi, speaking via Iranian state media, said the 'Islamabad Memorandum has never been closer' but is unsigned and could still change, describing a two-stage MoU and urging media to stop treating it as finalized. Iran's foreign ministry earlier called reports of a finalized agreement 'merely speculation' and said any deal must halt hostilities in Lebanon as well; officials reiterated they would not ship near-weapons-grade uranium abroad.

sources.title: Iran says no final decision made on deal Trump hopes could be signed soon
Iran's FM says ceasefire deal with US has 'never been closer'

stories.headline: Ukraine hits Russian oil terminals overnight as Putin admits the strikes are denting Russia's economy
stories.summary: Ukraine struck the Tamanneftgaz terminal in Krasnodar and an oil facility in Volgograd region overnight June 13, extending a 'Russia Day' raid the day before on Tatarstan and Samara plants, as fuel shortages and rationing spread to more than 25 Russian regions plus occupied Crimea. Russia's Energy Ministry acknowledged 'temporary complications' in fuel supplies and President Putin publicly conceded the deep-strike campaign is hitting Russia's economy and society — a rare admission.

perspectives.keyFraming: Putin's admission marks the first acknowledgment that the refinery campaign is producing real economic strain.
perspectives.summary: Al Jazeera and RFE/RL reported that Putin conceded Ukraine's attacks are affecting the Russian economy and society, with Moscow's Energy Ministry admitting 'temporary complications' in fuel supplies as drone hits on refinery hydrocrackers spread shortages to occupied Crimea and dozens of Russian regions.

sources.title: Putin admits Ukraine attacks hitting Russian economy, society
Drone Hits On Hydrocrackers Spark Fuel Crunch As Ukraine Pounds Russia's Refineries
Ukraine strikes fuel supplies to Crimea, sparking a fuel crisis

perspectives.keyFraming: Ukrainian sources cast the strikes as strategic leverage degrading Russia's war logistics.
perspectives.summary: The Kyiv Post reported Ukraine marked 'Russia Day' with a massive June 12 drone raid on key oil refineries in Tatarstan and Samara, part of a campaign of well over 150 refinery strikes since 2022 that has spread fuel shortages across 25-plus Russian regions, framing the deep strikes as a way to impose economic costs and pressure Moscow.

sources.title: Ukraine Marks Russia Day With Massive Drone Raid on Key Oil Refineries in Tatarstan and Samara

stories.headline: Russian overnight barrage kills at least 22 across Kyiv and Dnipro; Zelensky says strikes will continue without air defense
stories.summary: A large overnight Russian missile-and-drone attack killed at least 22 people — including children in Dnipro — and wounded more than 130, toppling an apartment building, with Ukraine reporting roughly 73 missiles and over 600 drones launched and most intercepted. Zelensky called it 'an explicit statement by Russia' that strikes will continue absent better missile defense, ahead of his push at the G7 for sanctions and air defenses. Trump suggested the strike set back peace efforts.

perspectives.keyFraming: Wire focus on the scale and civilian toll of one of the deadliest barrages in weeks, ahead of the G7.
perspectives.summary: PBS and CBS detailed at least 22 dead and a downed apartment block, with Ukraine citing dozens of missiles and hundreds of drones launched overnight and most intercepted, framing the assault as escalation amid stalled diplomacy.

sources.title: Massive Russian attack on Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities kills 22 people, officials say
Major Russian missile, drone attack kills at least 22, topples apartment building

perspectives.keyFraming: Zelensky ties the barrage to his G7 appeal for air defense and tougher Russia sanctions.
perspectives.summary: ABC News carried Zelensky's 'explicit statement' framing and his demand for protection from ballistic and cruise missiles, linking the strike to his case at the summit for new sanctions and use of frozen Russian assets.

sources.title: Russia launches 'horrific' drone, missile strikes on Ukraine, killing 22

perspectives.keyFraming: Russia frames intensified strikes as retaliation to deter Ukrainian attacks on its infrastructure.
perspectives.summary: Via TASS, Russian officials cast the wave of strikes as a response to Ukrainian attacks on Russian energy and civilian facilities, insisting Moscow would keep raising the cost of Kyiv's long-range campaign.

sources.title: Russia to increase retaliatory strikes on Ukrainian infrastructure facilities
